items.q: How do you see a non-luminous object like a book?
items.correct: Light from a source reflects off the book and enters your eyes.
items.misconception: Your eyes send out beams of vision that hit the book and let you see it.
items.random_wrong: The book emits a special invisible signal that your brain detects directly.
items.why: The 'extramission' model of vision is a stubborn childhood intuition, reinforced by phrases like 'cast your eye over' or comic-book vision rays.

items.q: If one EM wave has a higher frequency than another, what is true about its wavelength?
items.correct: It is shorter — wavelength and frequency are inversely related (c = f x lambda).
items.misconception: It is also longer, because both increase together.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged — only the speed differs.
items.why: Students treat all wave properties as moving together (more of one = more of the other), missing the fixed-speed constraint that ties them inversely.

items.q: When you push a wall, what does Newton's third law say about the wall?
items.correct: The wall exerts an equal and opposite force on you at the same instant.
items.misconception: The wall only pushes back if your force is strong enough to make it react.
items.random_wrong: The wall pushes back with a force proportional to how stiff it is, not equal to your force.
items.why: Students think action-reaction is causal and sequential — push first, then the wall 'responds' — instead of being two aspects of one interaction.

items.q: Angles in optics are measured from which line?
items.why: It's The normal. “The mirror surface” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a ray hits a flat mirror with an angle of incidence of 40°, what is the angle of reflection?
items.why: Correct answer: 40°. “20°” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A ray hits a mirror at an angle of incidence of 25°. What is the angle of reflection, in degrees?
items.why: The real figure is 25. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
